questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: What is the difference between row‑wise and column‑wise data selection in Unix?
questions.answer: Row‑wise selection chooses whole lines (e.g., head, tail, sed); column‑wise selection chooses fields from each line (e.g., cut, awk).
questions.explanation: Row‑wise operates on line boundaries; column‑wise splits each line into delimited fields.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: Which command is used to select the first 10 rows of a file?
questions.explanation: `head` displays the first N lines; `tail` shows the last N lines.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows 5 to 15 from a file?
questions.explanation: `sed` with the `p` command prints the address range; `head`+`tail` uses line count.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: What command selects the second column (field) from a space‑separated file?
questions.explanation: `cut` uses delimiter `-d` and field numbers; `awk` uses `$1`, `$2`, etc.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select all lines containing the word 'error' (row‑wise selection)?
questions.explanation: `grep` filters rows based on a pattern.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select column 3 and 5 from a CSV file (comma‑separated)?
questions.explanation: Field numbers can be given as a comma‑separated list or range (e.g., `-f3-5`).

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows where the second column is greater than 100 using `awk`?
questions.explanation: `awk` can perform conditional selection on fields.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select columns 2 to 4 from a tab‑separated file?
questions.explanation: The `-f2-4` syntax selects fields 2,3,4.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select every row except the header line?
questions.explanation: `tail -n +2` prints from line 2 onward; `sed '1d'` deletes line 1.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select lines that match a pattern and also a specific column?
questions.explanation: `awk` combines row selection (pattern) and column printing.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: What does `head -5 file.txt | cut -c1-10` do?
questions.answer: Selects first 10 characters of each of the first 5 lines.
questions.explanation: `-c` selects characters by position; pipes chain row and column operations.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows 50 to 100 using `sed`?
questions.explanation: `-n` suppresses default output; `p` prints only the range.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows ending with a digit?
questions.explanation: The regex `[0-9]$` matches lines ending with a digit.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select all columns except the first one using `cut`?
questions.explanation: `f2-` means from field 2 to the last field.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select every other row (odd rows)?
questions.explanation: `NR` is line number; modulo 2 selects odd lines.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows whose first column equals a given value using `awk`?
questions.explanation: `-F,` sets delimiter; string comparison in `awk`.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select columns by name (if header exists) using `awk`?
questions.answer: Read the header line into an array, then use the column index: `awk 'NR==1{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++)h[$i]=i} NR>1{print $h["price"]}' file.csv`.
questions.explanation: This associative array technique maps column names to field numbers.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows between two patterns (inclusive) with `sed`?
questions.explanation: The range syntax `/start/,/end/` selects lines between the first match of `start` and first match of `end`.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select columns where the column name matches a pattern?
questions.answer: Use `awk` with header line: `awk 'NR==1{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++)if($i~/pattern/)c[i]=1} NR>1{for(i in c)printf "%s%s", $i, (++j==length(c)?ORS:OFS)}'`.
questions.explanation: This prints only columns whose header matches the regex pattern.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows where any column contains a certain string?
questions.explanation: Loop through fields, print line and exit loop on first match.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows 10‑20 and also extract only columns 3 and 5 in one pipeline?
questions.explanation: Pipe row selection into column selection.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select random rows from a file?
questions.explanation: `shuf` randomly permutes lines; `-n` limits output.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows where the second field is numeric?
questions.answer: `awk '$2+0 == $2' file.txt` (checks if field2 is a number).
questions.explanation: Adding zero converts to number; if it remains same, it's numeric.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select columns based on a delimiter other than space/tab using `cut`?
questions.explanation: `-d:` sets delimiter to colon.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows that contain a pattern but print only a specific column?
questions.explanation: Grep filters rows, then cut extracts the column.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows with line numbers using `cat -n` and then cut?
questions.answer: `cat -n file.txt | cut -c1-6` (first 6 chars contain line numbers).
questions.explanation: `cat -n` prefixes line numbers; you can then cut those columns.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows that are not empty?
questions.explanation: `^$` matches empty lines; `-v` inverts the match.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select columns whose values are numbers > 100?
questions.explanation: Loop through fields and print only those meeting condition.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows where the length of the line is greater than 80 characters?
questions.explanation: `length($0)` returns number of characters in the whole line.

questions.topic: Row-wise and column-wise data selection
questions.question: How to select rows that are duplicated in a file (non‑unique rows)?
questions.explanation: First sort: `sort file.txt | uniq -d` prints lines that appear more than once.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: What is the default delimiter for the `cut` command?
questions.explanation: If `-d` is not specified, `cut` treats tabs as field separators.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut the first 5 characters from each line?
questions.explanation: `-c` selects character positions; `1-5` means columns 1 through 5.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut the third field from a colon‑separated file?
questions.explanation: `-d:` sets colon as delimiter; `-f3` selects the third field.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields 2, 4, and 6 from a CSV file?
questions.explanation: Comma‑separated field list without spaces.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: What does `cut -f1-3,5` mean?
questions.explanation: Hyphen denotes ranges; comma separates distinct ranges.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut characters from column 10 to the end of each line?
questions.explanation: `10-` means from position 10 to the end of the line.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: What is the purpose of `cut --complement`?
questions.answer: It selects the complement (opposite) of the specified fields or characters.
questions.explanation: Example: `cut -f1 --complement file.txt` prints all except field 1.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to change the output delimiter for `cut`?
questions.explanation: `--output-delimiter` changes the character between output fields.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields where lines may not have all fields?
questions.answer: `cut` outputs nothing for missing fields; it cannot pad. Use `awk` for robustness.
questions.explanation: `cut` expects each line to have at least the requested fields; missing fields produce empty output.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut multiple character ranges, e.g., characters 1‑5 and 10‑15?
questions.explanation: Ranges can be combined with commas; output concatenates the portions.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields but suppress lines with no delimiter?
questions.explanation: `-s` (suppress) omits lines that do not contain the delimiter.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: What happens when you use `cut -f2` on a tab‑separated file without `-d`?
questions.answer: It correctly selects the second tab‑delimited field (default delimiter is tab).
questions.explanation: The default `-d` is tab, so `-f2` works as expected.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields from standard input?
questions.answer: `cat file.txt | cut -d' ' -f1` works, or directly `cut -d' ' -f1 < file.txt`.
questions.explanation: `cut` reads from stdin if no file argument given.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to use `cut` with a delimiter that is a space but multiple spaces?
questions.answer: `cut` treats each space as a separate delimiter; it cannot handle multiple spaces as one. Use `tr -s ' ' | cut`.
questions.explanation: `tr -s` squeezes repeated spaces into single space.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut bytes instead of characters?
questions.answer: `cut -b 1-5 file.txt` (byte positions; differs from `-c` only for multi‑byte encodings).
questions.explanation: `-b` works on bytes; useful for binary files or single‑byte locales.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut characters until the first occurrence of a pattern (not supported by cut)?
questions.explanation: `cut` works only on fixed positions or delimiters, not regex patterns.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields from a file where delimiter is a colon but fields may be empty?
questions.answer: `cut -d: -f1,3 /etc/passwd` works even if some fields are empty; `cut` outputs empty strings.
questions.explanation: Empty fields are represented as consecutive delimiters.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields only if the line begins with a specific character?
questions.answer: `grep '^#' file.txt | cut -d' ' -f2` (use grep to filter first).
questions.explanation: Pipe row selection (`grep`) into column selection.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: How to cut fields while preserving the original delimiter in output?
questions.answer: `cut` by default outputs fields separated by the same delimiter (`-d`); use `--output-delimiter` to change.
questions.explanation: Default output delimiter matches input delimiter unless overridden.

questions.topic: Using cut command with different options
questions.question: What is the difference between `cut -c1-5` and `cut -b1-5` in UTF‑8 locale?
questions.answer: `-c` counts characters (multi‑byte), `-b` counts bytes (may split multi‑byte characters).
questions.explanation: For UTF‑8, `-c` is safer; `-b` can produce invalid bytes if cutting inside a multi‑byte codepoint.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: What does the `paste` command do?
questions.answer: It merges lines from multiple files horizontally, joining them side‑by‑side with a delimiter.
questions.explanation: `paste` writes corresponding lines from each file as separate columns.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste two files `a.txt` and `b.txt` into one output?
questions.explanation: Output: line1 of a.txt, then a tab, then line1 of b.txt, etc.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to change the delimiter from tab to a comma?
questions.explanation: `-d` specifies the delimiter character(s).

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste the same file multiple times to repeat its content?
questions.explanation: Each occurrence of the file creates an additional column.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: What does `paste -s` do?
questions.answer: Serial (column‑wise) pasting: combines all lines of each file into a single line.
questions.explanation: `-s` merges lines of a file horizontally, not across files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files with a newline as delimiter?
questions.answer: `paste -d'\n' file1 file2` (each line from each file appears on its own line).
questions.explanation: Using `\n` as delimiter interleaves lines vertically (like `cat` but interleaved).

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste three files into three columns?
questions.explanation: Number of columns equals number of input files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to use a different delimiter for each field (e.g., first field separated by colon, then space)?
questions.answer: `paste -d': \n' file1 file2 file3` cycles through the delimiters.
questions.explanation: Delimiters are used cyclically; here `:` between col1 & col2, space between col2 & col3, newline after col3.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: What does `paste -d'\0'` do?
questions.answer: Uses null character as delimiter, effectively concatenating lines with no separation.
questions.explanation: `\0` is the null byte; not recommended for text files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files when one file has fewer lines than the other?
questions.answer: `paste` uses empty strings for missing lines (no side‑by‑side lines become blank).
questions.explanation: The output line count equals the maximum number of lines among input files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste the output of commands instead of files?
questions.explanation: `<(command)` creates a temporary file descriptor that `paste` can read.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste horizontally with a tab delimiter but suppress the final tab?
questions.answer: `paste` naturally does not add trailing delimiter. It's not configurable.
questions.explanation: Last field has no trailing delimiter; you cannot change that.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files while also adding a header row?
questions.explanation: `-` reads standard input, allowing you to insert a header.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to transpose rows to columns using `paste`?
questions.answer: `paste -s -d'\t' file.txt` (serial mode with tab) turns rows into columns.
questions.explanation: `-s` reads all lines of a file and pastes them horizontally.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste two files with one field delimited by comma and the other by space?
questions.explanation: First delimiter `,` after file1, second delimiter ` ` after file2.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files and add a newline after every field?
questions.explanation: This effectively interleaves lines from the files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files where each file is generated from a pipeline?
questions.explanation: Process substitution allows using command outputs as virtual files.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to paste files with a delimiter that is tab but output with spaces?
questions.explanation: `paste` outputs tabs; `tr` replaces tabs with spaces.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: What is the difference between `paste` and `join`?
questions.answer: `paste` simply concatenates lines side‑by‑side; `join` aligns lines based on a common key field.
questions.explanation: `join` requires sorted input and matches keys; `paste` just lines up by line number.

questions.topic: Pasting and merging files horizontally using paste
questions.question: How to use `paste` to create a CSV from two lists?
questions.explanation: `-d,` sets comma as delimiter; each column corresponds to a file.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: What does the `split` command do?
questions.answer: Splits a file into smaller pieces, each of a specified size or number of lines.
questions.explanation: Creates files named `xaa`, `xab`, … by default.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file `big.txt` into pieces of 1000 lines each?
questions.explanation: `-l` specifies lines per output file.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file into pieces of 10 MB each?
questions.explanation: `-b` sets byte size; m = megabyte, k = kilobyte, g = gigabyte.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to change the prefix of output files (instead of `x`)?
questions.explanation: The last argument becomes the prefix.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file into pieces of a specific number of lines but also add a numeric suffix?
questions.explanation: `-d` uses numbers instead of letters: `part_00`, `part_01`.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file based on byte count while keeping lines intact?
questions.explanation: `-C` tries to split at line boundaries, not exceeding the size.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: What does `split -n 5 file.txt` do?
questions.answer: Splits the file into 5 roughly equal‑sized pieces (not necessarily exact line counts).
questions.explanation: `-n` number of output files (GNU extension).

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file but also keep the original file?
questions.answer: `split` always creates new files; the original is not removed.
questions.explanation: Unlike `gzip`, `split` leaves the input file unchanged.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file but number the output files from 1 instead of 00?
questions.explanation: `--numeric-suffixes=1` starts numbering at 1 (GNU `split`).

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file using a pattern as a record separator?
questions.answer: `csplit` (context split) is used for splitting based on patterns.
questions.explanation: `csplit file '/pattern/' {*}` splits at every occurrence of pattern.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file into exactly two pieces at a given line?
questions.explanation: `split` cannot split at a specific line number; use `head` and `tail`.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to rebuild the original file from split pieces?
questions.answer: `cat x?? > original` (if split with default prefix).
questions.explanation: `cat` concatenates the pieces in order (except `split -n` which may not be concatenable).

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a large file and compress each piece on the fly?
questions.explanation: `--filter` runs a command for each piece; `$FILE` is the output filename.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a binary file with a certain chunk size and run checksums?
questions.explanation: Split into 1 MB pieces; compute checksums for integrity.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: What is the difference between `split -n l/1` and normal split?
questions.answer: It's a specific chunk method (e.g., `split -n l/10` splits into 10 chunks by lines).
questions.explanation: GNU `split -n` supports chunking methods: `l` (line), `r` (round‑robin), `b` (byte).

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file into pieces of exactly 1000 bytes, discarding remainder?
questions.answer: `split -b 1000 -d file.bin part_`; the last piece may be smaller.
questions.explanation: `split` cannot truncate extra bytes; remainder goes into a smaller piece.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file into pieces with custom suffix length?
questions.explanation: `-a` sets the number of characters in the suffix.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file but name pieces by their MD5 sum?
questions.answer: Cannot directly; use a script: `split -l 1000 -d file part_` then rename each piece after its checksum.
questions.explanation: `split` does not have built‑in renaming based on content.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: How to split a file and count the number of pieces?
questions.explanation: Count the resulting files after splitting.

questions.topic: Splitting large files using split command
questions.question: What is the maximum number of pieces `split` can create with default letters?
questions.answer: 676 (aa to zz) if using two letters; with 3 letters, 17576, etc.
questions.explanation: Suffix length determines max pieces; `-a` increases capacity.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: What does `wc` stand for and what does it do?
questions.answer: Word Count – counts lines, words, characters (or bytes) in a file.
questions.explanation: Default output: lines, words, bytes (in that order).

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count only the number of lines in a file?
questions.explanation: `-l` displays only line count.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count only the number of words in a file?
questions.explanation: `-w` counts words (sequences of non‑whitespace characters).

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count only the number of characters (not bytes) in a file?
questions.explanation: `-m` counts characters (respects multi‑byte encodings).

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: What is the difference between `wc -c` and `wc -m` in UTF‑8?
questions.answer: `-c` counts bytes; `-m` counts characters (which can be multiple bytes).
questions.explanation: For ASCII they are identical; for non‑ASCII, `-m` is smaller.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count lines, words, and characters from standard input?
questions.explanation: `wc` reads stdin if no filename given.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the number of lines in all `.txt` files recursively?
questions.explanation: `find` runs `wc -l` on each file; `awk` sums the counts.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: What does `wc -l file.txt` output if the file does not end with newline?
questions.answer: It still counts lines correctly; a non‑newline‑terminated last line is not counted as a separate line.
questions.explanation: `wc` defines a line as ending with a newline character; non‑terminated text does not count as a line.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count words in a file ignoring punctuation?
questions.explanation: `tr` replaces non‑alphanumeric with newlines, then `wc -l` counts tokens.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the number of lines of output from a command?
questions.explanation: Pipes the output to `wc`.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the total number of characters across multiple files?
questions.answer: `wc -m file1 file2 file3 | tail -1` shows total.
questions.explanation: The last line of `wc` output with multiple files is the total sum.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count unique words in a file using `wc` and other commands?
questions.explanation: Split into words, sort, uniq, then count lines.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: What does `wc -L` do?
questions.answer: Prints the length of the longest line (in bytes).
questions.explanation: `-L` (GNU extension) displays the maximum line length.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the number of non‑empty lines?
questions.answer: `grep -c '.' file.txt` (lines with at least one character).
questions.explanation: `grep -c` counts matching lines; `'.'` matches non‑empty lines.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to get only the number of bytes (without filename)?
questions.explanation: Redirection causes `wc` to read from stdin, suppressing filename output.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count words in a file but treat hyphenated words as one word?
questions.answer: `wc -w` already treats hyphenated strings as a single word (since hyphens are not whitespace).
questions.explanation: Definition of a word is sequence of non‑whitespace characters.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the number of characters excluding newlines?
questions.explanation: Better: `tr -d '\n' < file.txt | wc -c`.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the number of occurrences of a pattern using `wc`?
questions.explanation: `grep -o` outputs each match on a separate line; `wc -l` counts them.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to count the total number of lines in all files in a directory?
questions.explanation: The last line of `wc -l *` is the total sum.

questions.topic: Counting lines, words, and characters using wc
questions.question: How to get word count of a string variable in a script?
questions.explanation: Pass the variable through `echo` to `wc`.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: What does the `sort` command do?
questions.answer: Sorts lines of a text file (alphabetically by default).
questions.explanation: Sort order is based on the current locale; typically dictionary order.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort a file `data.txt` alphabetically?
questions.explanation: Default sorting is ascending alphabetic.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort numerically (by numeric value) instead of alphabetical?
questions.explanation: `-n` sorts by arithmetic value (e.g., 2 before 10).

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort in reverse order?
questions.explanation: `-r` reverses the sorting order.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort a file and output the result to a new file?
questions.explanation: Redirection writes the sorted output.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort case‑insensitively?
questions.explanation: `-f` folds lower case to upper for comparison.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: What does `sort -u` do?
questions.answer: Sorts and removes duplicate lines (equivalent to `sort | uniq`).
questions.explanation: `-u` outputs only one copy of identical lines.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort by human‑readable numbers (e.g., 1K, 2M)?
questions.explanation: `-h` understands K, M, G, etc.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort file by month names (e.g., Jan, Feb)?
questions.explanation: Recognises three‑letter month abbreviations: JAN, FEB, …

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort a file ignoring leading whitespace?
questions.explanation: `-b` ignores leading blanks when comparing.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: What is the difference between `sort -n` and `sort -g`?
questions.answer: `-n` sorts by numerical value; `-g` uses general numerical sort (handles scientific notation).
questions.explanation: `-g` is slower and supports floating‑point and exponential notation.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort and check if the file is already sorted?
questions.explanation: `-c` does not output anything, just exit code.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort using a different character as field separator?
questions.explanation: `-t` sets the field delimiter; `-k` selects a field.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort by the second character of each line?
questions.explanation: `k1.2` means start at character 2 of field 1, end at character 2.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort by last field (variable number of fields)?
questions.answer: `rev file | sort | rev` (reverse, sort, reverse back).
questions.explanation: `rev` reverses each line, making last field first; then sort, then reverse again.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort using a dictionary order that ignores punctuation?
questions.explanation: `-d` considers only letters, digits, and blanks.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort a file in place (modify original)?
questions.explanation: `-o` allows overwriting the input file; safer than redirect.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort large files that don't fit in memory?
questions.explanation: `-S` specifies memory buffer; `--parallel` uses multiple threads.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: How to sort numbers that include decimal points (e.g., 1.2, 10.1)?
questions.answer: `sort -n` works correctly for decimal numbers (as long as they are aligned).
questions.explanation: Sort treats '.' as a character; numerical sort works with decimal numbers as floating point.

questions.topic: Sorting file content (alphabetical and numerical sorting)
questions.question: What is the difference between `sort -k2,2` and `sort -k2`?
questions.answer: `-k2,2` sorts only by the second field; `-k2` sorts by second field to the end of the line.
questions.explanation: Specifying a stop position (end) limits the sort key.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort a CSV file by the third column?
questions.explanation: `-t,` sets comma delimiter; `-k3` uses third field as key.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by second column numerically?
questions.explanation: `k2n` means field 2, numeric sort.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by first column in reverse order?
questions.explanation: `r` after the key specifies reverse order for that key.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by multiple columns: first by column 2, then by column 1?
questions.explanation: Multiple `-k` options define primary, secondary, etc. sort order.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: What is the default field separator for `sort -k`?
questions.explanation: By default, fields are separated by any amount of whitespace.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by column 3, but treat column 3 as number with decimal?
questions.explanation: `-n` works for decimal numbers; for scientific notation use `-g`.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by column 2 (numeric) and then column 3 (alphabetical)?
questions.explanation: Primary numeric on col2; secondary alphabetic on col3.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by a column that uses pipe (`|`) as delimiter?
questions.explanation: The delimiter character must be quoted to avoid shell interpretation.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by column 2, but only consider characters 2‑5 of that column?
questions.explanation: `k2.2,2.5` means start at character 2 of field 2, stop at character 5.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by the second column, case‑insensitively?
questions.explanation: Flags like `f`, `n`, `r` can be attached to a key.

questions.topic: Sorting based on specific columns
questions.question: How to sort by a column that may have missing values?
questions.answer: `sort -s -k2 file.txt` (stable sort) keeps original order for equal keys.
questions.explanation: Missing fields sort before non‑missing (empty fields are smaller).

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: What does the `diff` command do?
questions.answer: Compares two files line by line and shows differences.
questions.explanation: Output indicates which lines need to be added, removed, or changed to make files identical.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to compare two files `a.txt` and `b.txt`?
questions.explanation: The output uses `a` and `b` to denote the files.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to produce a unified diff (context lines)?
questions.explanation: Unified format shows a few lines before and after changes.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to ignore case differences while comparing?
questions.explanation: `-i` treats uppercase and lowercase as equal.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: What does `diff -q` do?
questions.answer: Reports only whether files differ (no details).
questions.explanation: `-q` quiet mode; exits with 0 if identical, 1 if different.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to ignore whitespace differences?
questions.explanation: Also `-b` ignores changes in amount of whitespace; `-B` ignores blank lines.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to compare two directories recursively?
questions.explanation: `-r` compares all files in subdirectories.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: What does `diff -y` do?
questions.explanation: `-y` shows the two files next to each other with markers for differences.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to create a patch file using `diff`?
questions.explanation: Unified diff is most common for patches.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to compare only the first 50 lines of two files?
questions.explanation: `<(command)` creates a temporary file descriptor.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: What does `diff -Z` do?
questions.explanation: Similar to `-b` but more specific.

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to use `diff` with a third file as a context?
questions.answer: `diff` only compares two files. For three‑way merge, use `diff3`.
questions.explanation: `diff3` compares three files (typically original, modified1, modified2).

questions.topic: Comparing files line by line using diff
questions.question: How to ignore lines matching a regular expression?
questions.explanation: The `-I` option ignores lines that match the regex when comparing.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: What does the `cmp` command do?
questions.answer: Compares two files byte by byte (character by character).
questions.explanation: `cmp` outputs the first position where the files differ.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: How to compare two files with `cmp`?
questions.explanation: If identical, no output; otherwise prints byte and line number of first difference.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: How to get a verbose output with byte and line numbers?
questions.answer: `cmp -l file1 file2` prints byte numbers and differing bytes.
questions.explanation: `-l` outputs each difference with offset and byte values (octal).

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: What does `cmp -s` do?
questions.answer: Silent mode; only returns exit code (0 if same, 1 if different, 2 if error).
questions.explanation: Useful in scripts to check equality without output.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: How to limit the comparison to the first N bytes?
questions.explanation: `-n` sets the number of bytes to compare.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: What is the difference between `cmp` and `diff`?
questions.answer: `cmp` works at byte level (binary); `diff` works line‑oriented (text).
questions.explanation: `cmp` is faster for binary files; `diff` is for human‑readable text changes.

questions.topic: Comparing files character by character using cmp
questions.question: How to compare two files and ignore the first 1KB?
questions.explanation: `-i OFFSET1:OFFSET2` skips early bytes.

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: What does `comm` do?
questions.answer: Compares two sorted files line by line and outputs three columns: lines only in file1, lines only in file2, lines common to both.
questions.explanation: Input files must be sorted (use `sort` beforehand).

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: How to show only lines common to both files?
questions.explanation: `-1` supress first column, `-2` suppress second column, `-3` suppress third column.

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: How to show lines that are in file1 but not in file2?
questions.explanation: Suppresses column 2 and 3.

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: How to suppress the tab separators in `comm` output?
questions.answer: `comm` always uses tabs; to remove them, pipe through `tr -d '\t'`.
questions.explanation: Alternatively, use `join` or `awk` for custom formatting.

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: How to compare files that are not sorted?
questions.answer: `comm` will produce incorrect output; you must sort them first: `comm <(sort file1) <(sort file2)`.
questions.explanation: Process substitution sorts on the fly.

questions.topic: Comparing sorted files using comm
questions.question: How to use `comm` to find lines that occur only in one file ignoring case?
questions.answer: Use `tr` to convert case then sort: `comm <(tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' < file1 | sort) <(tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' < file2 | sort)`.
questions.explanation: The output will show case‑insensitive uniqueness.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: What does the `join` command do?
questions.answer: Joins lines of two sorted files based on a common key field (like SQL join).
questions.explanation: By default, join uses the first field as key and whitespace as separator.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to join two files on the first field?
questions.answer: `join file1 file2` (both must be sorted on the key).
questions.explanation: Output contains the common key followed by remaining fields from both files.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to join on a different field (e.g., second field of first file and third of second)?
questions.explanation: `-1` specifies field number in first file; `-2` in second file.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to change the field separator to colon?
questions.explanation: Delimiter must be the same for both files.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: What does `join -v 1` do?
questions.answer: Outputs lines from the first file that have no match in the second (unmatched lines).
questions.explanation: `-v 1` prints only unpaired lines from file1; `-v 2` from file2.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to join on a field but suppress the key from output?
questions.explanation: `-o` specifies which fields to output; key field can be omitted.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to perform an outer join (include all lines from both files)?
questions.explanation: `-a1` includes unpairable lines from file1; `-a2` from file2.

questions.topic: Joining two files using join command
questions.question: How to join files where some lines have empty join field?
questions.answer: `join` treats empty field as empty key; lines with empty key will match if both empty. Use `-a` to include them.
questions.explanation: Empty field may cause unintended matches; consider preprocessing.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: What does `uniq` do?
questions.answer: Removes duplicate consecutive lines from a sorted file.
questions.explanation: It works only on adjacent duplicates; input must be sorted for full effect.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to remove all duplicate lines from a file?
questions.explanation: Use `sort` first to bring identical lines together.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to count occurrences of each line?
questions.explanation: `-c` prefixes each line with its count.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to print only duplicate lines?
questions.explanation: `-d` prints lines that appear more than once (one copy).

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to print only unique lines (non‑duplicate)?
questions.explanation: `-u` prints lines that appear exactly once.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to ignore the first N characters when comparing lines?
questions.explanation: `-s` specifies number of characters to skip.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to use `uniq` on a field instead of the whole line?
questions.explanation: `uniq` works on entire line; you must preprocess to extract fields.

questions.topic: Removing duplicates using uniq command
questions.question: How to compare only a certain number of characters from the end?
questions.answer: `rev file | uniq -s 5 | rev` (reverse, skip from end).
questions.explanation: `rev` reverses each line; `-s` skips from the beginning of the reversed line.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: What does `tr` do?
questions.answer: Translates, squeezes, or deletes characters from standard input.
questions.explanation: `tr` is a character‑based filter, not line‑based.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to convert lowercase to uppercase?
questions.explanation: `tr` maps each character from the first set to the corresponding character in the second set.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to delete all digits from a text?
questions.explanation: `-d` deletes any character in the given set.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to squeeze multiple spaces into a single space?
questions.explanation: `-s` squeezes repeated characters into one.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to replace all newlines with spaces?
questions.explanation: This joins all lines into one line, separated by spaces.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to complement a character set (e.g., keep only digits)?
questions.explanation: `-c` complement; `-d` delete; together keep only the specified set.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to translate characters with ranges, like 'a-f' to 'A-F'?
questions.explanation: Ranges are defined by the first and last character with hyphen.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to replace a set of characters with another set where sets are different lengths?
questions.answer: The second set is padded with its last character to match the length of the first set.
questions.explanation: Example: `tr 'abc' 'xy'` maps a→x, b→y, c→y.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to remove non‑printable characters from a file?
questions.answer: `tr -cd '\11\12\15\40-\176' < file.txt` (keeps tab, newline, carriage return, space, and printable ASCII).
questions.explanation: Octal ranges can be used; `-cd` deletes complement.

questions.topic: Transformation of text using tr command
questions.question: How to convert a file to uppercase and also squeeze blank lines?
questions.explanation: `tr` only handles characters, not lines; combine with other commands.
